{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This affects the core promise made to customers about when they will receive their goods — one of the most critical touchpoints in the logistics domain.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Tracking / Customer-Facing ETA",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "ETA", "definition": "Estimated Time of Arrival — the promised delivery window communicated to the recipient" },
      { "term": "Exact Timestamp ETA", "definition": "An ETA expressed as a specific date and time (e.g., '14:30 on Friday')" },
      { "term": "Date-Only Window", "definition": "An ETA expressed as an entire calendar day without a specific time (e.g., 'by Friday')" },
      { "term": "Delivery Promise", "definition": "The commitment shown to the customer representing when their package will arrive — sets expectations and drives satisfaction" },
      { "term": "SLA", "definition": "Service Level Agreement — contractual delivery commitments, often with financial penalties for breach" },
      { "term": "Last Mile", "definition": "The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to the recipient's address" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A delivery ETA must always be communicated to the recipient once a shipment is in transit",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Removing ETA entirely would break the core tracking contract. The customer must always know when to expect delivery.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every shipment in 'in_transit' or later status has a non-null ETA visible to the recipient"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The ETA precision level must not violate any active SLA that specifies time-of-day commitments",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If contractual SLAs with B2B customers specify time-window delivery (e.g., 'before 12:00 noon'), downgrading to date-only would breach the contract and expose the company to penalties.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any shipment governed by an SLA with a time-of-day clause, the ETA retains time-level precision"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "ETA changes (re-estimates) must be communicated to the customer when the delivery date itself changes",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A date-only ETA that silently shifts from Friday to Monday without notification is a broken promise. State integrity requires the customer see the change.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a date-only ETA's date value changes, a notification event is emitted to the recipient"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Premium/express service tiers may require time-level ETA precision as part of their value proposition",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a product policy — a VP could decide to change what precision premium tiers offer. But it is a strong business expectation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Shipments with service_tier = 'express' or 'premium' retain time-of-day ETA precision unless the product team explicitly opts them into date-only"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Date-only ETAs mean 'by end of business day' on the stated date, not midnight",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The definition of the cutoff is a business policy, but it must be explicitly defined — ambiguity here causes operational and customer confusion.",
      "testable_assertion": "'Delivery by Friday' is satisfied if delivered by the latest delivery attempt time on Friday (e.g., 20:00 local time), not 23:59"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A shipment is in transit with an internally calculated ETA of '14:30 on Friday'",
      "when": "The customer views the tracking page",
      "then": "The ETA is displayed as 'Delivery by Friday' (date-only, no time component)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A shipment is governed by a B2B SLA with a time-window commitment (e.g., 'by 12:00 noon')",
      "when": "The ETA display logic runs",
      "then": "The time-level precision is preserved and the SLA time is shown, NOT downgraded to date-only"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A shipment's date-only ETA changes from Friday to Monday",
      "when": "The re-estimation occurs",
      "then": "The customer is notified of the date change via all configured notification channels"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A shipment has a premium/express service tier",
      "when": "The ETA display logic runs",
      "then": "The system checks the service tier's ETA precision policy before deciding whether to show date-only or time-level ETA"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "Internal systems (routing, dispatch, driver apps) consume the ETA",
      "when": "The ETA format change is applied",
      "then": "Internal systems continue to receive full timestamp precision — only customer-facing display is affected"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "Historical reporting and analytics pipelines consume ETA data",
      "when": "The format change is deployed",
      "then": "The internal ETA timestamp is still stored at full precision; the date-only rendering is a presentation-layer concern only"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Shipment crosses a timezone boundary — 'Friday' in which timezone?", "expected_behavior": "Date-only ETA must be expressed in the recipient's local timezone, not the sender's or the system's UTC." },
    { "scenario": "ETA is near midnight — internally 23:45 Friday vs 00:15 Saturday", "expected_behavior": "If the internal ETA is 00:15 Saturday, the date-only display must say 'Saturday', not 'Friday'. Rounding toward the earlier date would be a false promise." },
    { "scenario": "Same-day delivery — ETA is later today", "expected_behavior": "'Delivery by Today' is ambiguous. Same-day scenarios may still require time-level precision to be meaningful (e.g., 'by 6 PM today')." },
    { "scenario": "Multi-day shipping where ETA spans a weekend", "expected_behavior": "If delivery cannot occur on weekends, 'Delivery by Friday' shifting to 'Delivery by Monday' must be handled — not silently displayed as 'Saturday' if no weekend delivery exists." },
    { "scenario": "Customer has scheduled a specific delivery time slot", "expected_behavior": "If the customer booked a time slot (e.g., '10:00-12:00'), displaying only the date contradicts the customer's own selection. Scheduled deliveries must retain time precision." },
    { "scenario": "API consumers (partners, marketplaces) that parse the ETA programmatically", "expected_behavior": "API response schema changes from timestamp to date-only is a breaking change. Must be versioned or made backwards-compatible with a new field." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Moderate negative risk. If B2B SLAs require time-precision and the system loses it, penalty clauses could be triggered. Conversely, date-only ETAs reduce the surface area for 'late' deliveries — a package arriving at 15:00 instead of the promised 14:30 is no longer visibly late, potentially reducing refund/credit claims. Net financial impact depends heavily on the ratio of B2C (likely positive) vs B2B-SLA (potentially negative) shipments.",
    "operational": "Positive for operations. Exact-time ETAs create pressure on dispatch and routing to hit precise windows, increasing operational cost and driver stress. However, internal systems MUST retain full precision; this is a presentation change only.",
    "user": "Mixed. B2C customers lose the ability to plan their day around a specific time, which may increase 'where is my package?' support contacts. Industry trend (Amazon, FedEx) is toward time windows rather than exact times, so customer expectations are evolving. Same-day and premium customers will likely react negatively to losing time precision.",
    "risk": "High risk if applied uniformly without carve-outs. Key risks: (1) Breaking B2B SLA contracts — legal/financial exposure. (2) API breaking change for integration partners.       { "group": "Operations / Dispatch", "impact": "Positive — more routing flexibility, reduced pressure to hit exact time windows." },
      { "group": "Drivers / Couriers", "impact": "Positive — less pressure to hit exact timestamps reduces stress and unsafe driving behavior." },
      { "group": "API Integration Partners (Marketplaces, 3PLs)", "impact": "Breaking change if ETA field format changes. Must be managed via API versioning." },

      { "group": "Finance / Legal", "impact": "Must review all active SLAs for time-precision clauses before this change ships." }
